question_title: What will the US April 2026 nonfarm payrolls figure be in the initial release?
summary:
My forecast for US April 2026 nonfarm payrolls centers on approximately +83k jobs, slightly above the economist consensus of 60-75k but below the Kalshi prediction market implied median of ~99k.

Key reasoning: The labor market is in a "low-hire, low-fire" equilibrium. Initial jobless claims at a 56-year low (189k) indicate minimal layoffs, but ISM employment indices are deeply contractionary (manufacturing 46.4, services 45.2 in March), and JOLTS hiring is at pandemic lows. March's +178k was partly boosted by ~35k healthcare strike returns that won't repeat. The 2025 benchmark revision revealed the underlying trend is much weaker than initially reported (~17k/month vs ~103k/month initially).

The distribution is wide (80% CI: [0, 164]) reflecting extreme recent volatility (Jan +130k initial, Feb -92k initial, Mar +178k) and elevated policy uncertainty from tariffs, DOGE federal workforce reductions, and the Iran conflict's economic effects. I'm slightly more bearish than the Kalshi market in the right tail because ISM employment weakness in both manufacturing and services suggests hiring momentum has slowed, even if layoffs remain suppressed.

factors.description: Economist consensus clusters at 60-75k (Trading Economics ~70k, Investing.com ~73k), reflecting expectations of moderation after March's one-off healthcare strike return boost
Kalshi prediction market implies mean ~99k with std ~84k, more bullish than consensus. P(>80k)=56%, P(>100k)=47%. Market aggregates many informed participants
March +178k was partly one-off: healthcare strike returns added ~35k in physicians' offices. This won't repeat in April, creating mean-reversion pressure toward lower numbers
ISM Manufacturing Employment at 46.4 in April - deeply contracting, worst of 2026, 15th consecutive contraction month. Signals factory-sector employment weakness
ISM Services Employment tumbled to 45.2 in March - one of steepest drops since pandemic, reversing 3 months of growth. Services sector pulling back on hiring
Initial jobless claims at 189k for week ending April 25 - lowest since September 1969. 4-week average at 207.5k. Extraordinarily low layoffs signal labor market resilience
JOLTS shows hiring at lowest level since pandemic despite low layoffs - 'low-hire, low-fire' equilibrium limits upside for net job creation
2025 benchmark revision: only ~200k total jobs for the full year (~17k/month), down from ~103k/month initially reported. Underlying employment trend much weaker than it appeared
Historical NFP surprise std dev is ~80-100k in uncertain periods, with fat tails and ~55-60% positive surprise rate. Creates wide uncertainty envelope
Tariff headwinds: 189.6k blue-collar jobs lost since Liberation Day (April 2025). Manufacturing (-89k) and transportation/warehousing (-123.7k) heavily impacted
ADP weekly NER Pulse shows ~39.25k/week average through mid-April (preliminary), suggesting moderate but not weak private hiring momentum

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary: US April 2026 Nonfarm Payrolls

**Median estimate: +83k** (80% CI: [0, 164])

### Key Findings

| Percentile | Value (thousands) |
|-----------|------------------|
| P5 | -28 |
| P10 | 0 |
| P25 | 41 |
| P50 (median) | 83 |
| P75 | 124 |
| P90 | 164 |
| P95 | 191 |

### Core Reasoning

The labor market is stuck in a **"low-hire, low-fire" equilibrium**:

**Bullish signals:**
- Initial jobless claims at **189k** (lowest since 1969) — companies aren't laying workers off
- Kalshi market implies median ~99k, more optimistic than Wall Street consensus

**Bearish signals:**
- ISM Manufacturing Employment at **46.4** (worst of 2026, 15th straight contraction)
- ISM Services Employment collapsed to **45.2** in March
- JOLTS hiring at pandemic lows — companies aren't adding workers either
- March's +178k was inflated by ~35k healthcare strike returns (one-off)
- 2025 benchmark revision revealed underlying trend was only ~17k/month

My median of +83k sits between the economist consensus (60-75k) and the Kalshi market median (~99k), reflecting the tension between extraordinarily low layoffs and deeply contractionary hiring sentiment. The wide distribution (80% CI spanning 164k) captures the extreme recent volatility in this series.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) What will the US April 2026 nonfarm payrolls figure be in the initial release?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43370)

[April Jobs Report Forecasts Show Stable Growth, but Impact From Federal Layoffs and Tariffs Looms](https://www.morningstar.com/markets/april-jobs-report-forecasts-show-stable-growth-impact-federal-layoffs-tariffs-looms)
[US nonfarm payrolls for April 2026 Odds and Forecast | Octagon AI](https://octagonai.co/markets/economics/jobs-economy/us-nonfarm-payrolls-for-april-2026/)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves to the change in total nonfarm payroll employment (seasonally adjusted) for April 2026, in thousands of jobs, as first reported by the Bureau of Labor Statistics in the Employment Situation news release scheduled for May 8, 2026.

The figure will be taken from the lead summary of the BLS Employment Situation Summary published at https://www.bls.gov/news.release/empsit.nr0.htm (the headline sentence of the form "Total nonfarm payroll employment [rose/fell] by X in April"). Report the value in thousands — for example, a reported gain of 73,000 jobs resolves as **73**; a reported loss of 50,000 jobs resolves as **−50**.

Only the initial release counts; subsequent revisions in later monthly reports are ignored. If the May 8, 2026 release is delayed, the value from the first BLS Employment Situation release covering April 2026 reference-month data published before May 17, 2026 will be used. If no such release occurs before May 17, 2026, the question resolves N/A.
fine_print: If the BLS website is temporarily unavailable, the same figure as reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) citing the BLS Employment Situation release will be used.

factors.description: Economist consensus clusters at 60-75k (Trading Economics ~70k, Investing.com ~73k)
Kalshi prediction market implies mean ~99k with std ~84k, more bullish than consensus. P(>80k)=56%, median around 90k
March +178k was partly one-off: healthcare strike returns added ~35k to physicians offices. This won't repeat in April, creating mean-reversion pressure
ISM Manufacturing Employment at 46.4 in April - deeply contracting, worst of 2026, 15th consecutive contraction month
ISM Services Employment at 45.2 in March - one of steepest drops since pandemic, signals services sector pulling back on hiring
Initial jobless claims at 189k for week ending April 25 - lowest since September 1969, 4-week avg 207.5k. Very low layoffs

input.anchor: Consensus forecast of 70k (midpoint of 60-75k range from Trading Economics and Investing.com)
input.assessment:
My central estimate is ~83k, slightly above the economist consensus of 60-75k but below the Kalshi market-implied median of ~90-100k.

Key tensions:
1. Initial claims are extraordinarily low (189k, lowest since 1969) suggesting minimal layoffs → bullish for payrolls
2. But ISM employment indices are deeply contractionary in both manufacturing (46.4) and services (45.2) → bearish, suggesting hiring freezes
3. March's +178k was inflated by ~35k healthcare strike returns that won't repeat
4. The 2025 benchmark revision showed initial reports systematically overstated employment
5. Kalshi market is more bullish than economist consensus, particularly in the right tail

The "low-hire, low-fire" dynamic is key: companies aren't laying off workers but also aren't hiring, which could produce a modest positive print. The center of 80-85k seems reasonable as a compromise between the very bullish claims data and the bearish ISM employment/JOLTS hiring signals.

Distribution width: My 80% CI of [0, 164] spans 164k, which is reasonable given recent NFP volatility (3-month std dev of initial releases = 118k) and the historical surprise std dev of ~85-100k in uncertain periods. The Kalshi-implied 80% CI is wider at about 216k.
input.calibration_notes: My median (83k) is about 13k above the economist consensus center (70k), driven primarily by very low initial claims and slight positive surprise bias. My distribution is slightly narrower and lower than the Kalshi market (median 90-100k), which I believe is justified by ISM employment weakness. The 80% CI of [0, 164] captures a wide range from flat to March-like gains.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. Whether ISM employment weakness translates to actual payroll weakness (diffusion indices vs levels)
2. How much of March's strength was truly one-off vs. underlying improvement
3. Impact of DOGE federal workforce reductions on April government employment
4. Whether the claims signal (very bullish) or the ISM/JOLTS signal (bearish) is more predictive
5. Possible seasonal adjustment issues given the unusual recent data volatility
input.tool_audit:
Useful: research() sub-agent provided extensive labor market data including ISM, claims, JOLTS, Challenger data. web_search found Kalshi market-implied probabilities and ISM April manufacturing data. execute_code ran Monte Carlo simulations and fit Kalshi-implied distribution.

Empty/Limited: First research sub-agent couldn't access tools (fell back gracefully). Kalshi direct market API didn't return NFP-specific contracts (had to use Octagon AI analysis).

No failures.
input.update_triggers:
- ADP April report (May 6): if significantly above/below 70k would shift estimate
- ISM Services April report (May 5): employment sub-index would be very informative
- Any major company announcement of mass layoffs in April
- Any revision to March data released alongside April data
input.process_reflection:
The research() tool was very effective for this question - the Opus sub-agent compiled a comprehensive picture of the labor market. The web_search tool effectively found Kalshi market probabilities via Octagon AI when direct Kalshi didn't return results. The execute_code sandbox worked well for Monte Carlo simulation. 

One friction point: I couldn't find the ADP April monthly report (not yet released as of May 4), and the ADP weekly NER Pulse data was only available through mid-April in snippets. Having a dedicated economic calendar tool that shows which reports are already out vs pending would be useful.

Also, the Kalshi market contracts for NFP would be more useful if there were a dedicated prediction market tool that could pull structured contract data rather than relying on web scraping.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Resolution criteria**: Present, correctly sourced (BLS Employment Situation Summary for April 2026, headline number in thousands), and properly engaged with by the factors and assessment. ✓

**Factor-by-factor evidence check**:

1. **Factor 1 (Consensus 60-75k)**: Supported by trace — Trading Economics snippet (line 316-317) shows March +178k beat consensus of 60k; Metaculus question background (line 47) cites "Trading Economics ~70k; Investing.com ~73k." ✓

2. **Factor 2 (Kalshi ~99k mean)**: Supported — Octagon AI fetch (line 647-654) provides Kalshi contract prices. Agent's parametric fit yields mean=98.6k, std=84.2k (line 754). The factor's "P(>80k)=56%, median around 90k" matches the Octagon data. ✓

3. **Factor 3 (Healthcare strike returns)**: Supported — Trading Economics (line 316, 520) confirms "+35K in offices of physicians as workers returned from a strike." ✓

4. **Factor 4 (ISM Mfg Employment 46.4)**: Supported — Multiple search results (lines 404-437) confirm ISM Manufacturing Employment at 46.4 in April 2026, "15th consecutive contraction, the lowest this year." ✓

5. **Factor 5 (ISM Services Employment 45.2)**: Supported — Trading Economics (line 581-582) confirms "tumbled 6.6 points to 45.2 in March 2026, one of the steepest drops since the pandemic." Note: this is March data, not April (April ISM Services not yet released). Factor correctly states "in March." ✓

6. **Factor 6 (Initial claims 189k)**: Supported — Research sub-agent data (line 109-110) confirms "Initial claims: 189,000 — the lowest since September 1969," 4-week avg 207,500. ✓

7. **Factor 7 (JOLTS low hiring)**: Supported — Research data (line 113-115) confirms "Hiring fell to the lowest level since the pandemic" and "low-hire, low-fire" paradox. ✓

8. **Factor 8 (2025 revised to ~200k total)**: Supported — Research data (line 108) confirms "employers added fewer than 200,000 jobs in all of 2025." ✓

9. **Factor 9 (Historical surprise std dev)**: Supported — Research sub-agent's historical analysis (lines 203-258) provides detailed statistics: 70-85k std dev in normal times, 90-120k in high uncertainty, 55-60% positive surprise rate. ✓

10. **Factor 10 (189.6k blue-collar jobs lost)**: Supported — Research data (lines 103, 119, 184) from Center for American Progress confirms "189,600 blue-collar jobs lost since Liberation Day." ✓

11. **Factor 11 (ADP weekly NER Pulse ~39.25k/week)**: Supported — Trading Economics search result (line 366-368) confirms "employers added an average of 39,250 jobs per week in the four weeks ending April 11, 2026." ✓

**Double-counting check**: Mild overlap between Factor 1 (consensus) and Factor 3 (March strike one-off), since the consensus of 60-75k likely already incorporates the expected mean-reversion from March's strike-boosted number. However, both factors have moderate logit weights and the overlap is not severe enough to materially distort the forecast.

**Wrong-direction factors**: None found. All bearish factors have negative logits, all bullish factors have positive logits.

**Contradictory assessment**: The assessment's center of 83k is appropriately between the consensus anchor (70k) and the Kalshi market signal (99k), consistent with the factor balance.

**Counterargument quality**: Strong — identifies specific ADP NER Pulse implications (~169k monthly), the extraordinary claims data, March ex-strikes still at 143k, and seasonal construction strength. Constructs a coherent alternative narrative for 100-110k. Includes specific falsification conditions (ADP April report, ISM Services April, Kalshi movement). ✓

**Confidence assessment**: 0.35 is appropriate given the 118k std dev of recent initial releases vs. the agent's 69k distribution std dev. The agent acknowledges substantial uncertainty.

**Anchor divergence**: 13k departure from 70k anchor to 83k center (~19% of distribution std dev). Well-justified by the Kalshi market signal and claims data strength. ✓

**Probability assessment**: Center of 83k with 80% CI [0, 164] is reasonable. The distribution is somewhat narrower than what recent volatility (118k std dev) would suggest, but this is explicitly acknowledged. The center is a defensible compromise between the economist consensus (60-75k) and the Kalshi market-implied distribution (mean ~99k). I see no meaningful divergence from what the evidence supports.

**Tool audit**: Agent says "No failures" and metrics show 0.0% error rate across 92 calls — consistent. The first research sub-agent's lack of tool access is accurately described as a graceful fallback rather than a failure. ✓

No errors found that would change the forecast direction or meaningfully alter the probability.
